result:
I've been thinking about the strange beauty of uncertainty lately.

There's something deeply human about standing at the edge of what we know, peering into the fog of what we don't. Every scientific breakthrough began with someone saying "I don't know, but what if..." Every work of art emerged from the terrifying blank canvas, the empty page, the silence before the first note.

We often treat uncertainty as something to be conquered, a problem to be solved. But what if it's actually one of our greatest gifts? The fact that we can't predict everything, that surprise still exists, that tomorrow might unfold in ways we never imagined—isn't there something precious in that?

I find myself fascinated by the moments when people embrace not knowing: the pause before someone tries to explain a dream that's already fading, the split second before tasting something completely new, the way children ask "why?" about things adults have long stopped questioning.

Even in conversation, the most interesting moments often come from admitting ignorance rather than feigning expertise. "I never thought about it that way" might be one of the most generous things we can say to each other.

Perhaps wisdom isn't about accumulating certainties but about becoming more comfortable with mystery, more skilled at dancing with the unknown. After all, if we knew everything, if all questions had answers, what would be left to talk about under the stars?
